IS 2116 : 1980 SAND FOR MASONRY MORTARS
(First Revison)

1. Scope — Requirements of naturally occurring 2.2 Deleterious Material — The sand shall not contain
sands, crushed stone sands and crushed gravel sands any harmful impurities such as iron pyrites, alkalis, salts,
used in mortars for construction of masonry. coal or other organic impurities, mica, shale or similar

laminated materials, soft fragments, sea shells in such
2. Requirements form or in such quantities as to affect adversely the

hardening, strength or durability of the mortar.
2.1 General — The sand shall be hard, durable, clean 2.3 Limits of Deleterious Material — The maximum
and free from adherent coatings and organic matter and quantities of clay, fine silt, fine dust and organic impurities
shall not contain the amount of clay, silt and fine dust in the sand shall not exceed the following limits:
more than specified in 2.3 (a).

a)    Clay, fine silt and fine dust
          1) In natural sand or crushed gravel sand Not more than 5 percent by mass
           2) In crushed stone sand Not more than 5 percent by mass

b)     Organic impurities. Colour of the liquid shall be  lighter than
that indicated by the standard solution.

2.4. Grading
     (a)  The particle size grading of sand for use in mortars shall be within the limits as specified  below:

IS Sieve Designation Percentage Passing by Mass

surface for the test. The behaviour and performance of these coal   dispersion pitches are functions of the property of the body
of the material and not of any surface skin. To overcome this, fill the cup up to the brim and after cooling down, pour a little
excess of material slowly and  carefully to form  a convex surface. The excess material is to be cut with a hot knife after cooling
for 15 min  at room  temperature.

IS 2556 (PART 2) : 1994 VITREOUS SANITARY APPLIANCES
(VITREOUS CHINA) PART 2 - SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS OF

WASHDOWN WATER CLOSETS
(Fourth Revision)

1. Scope — Requirements for patterns, construction, 5.4  Single Ball Test —  The ball shall be discharged in
dimensions and tolerances, finish and marking for the normal manner.
vitreous washdown water closets (henceforth referred
as WC). 5.5 Fifty Ball Test —  A minimum of 85 percent of all

balls should be flushed out in the five tests.
2. Patterns

a) Pattern 1  Pedestal WC with  and  indepen-
dent cistern

    Pattern 2  (See Fig. 1)
b) Pattern 3  Pedestal WC with  independent cis-

tern and  horizontal outlet (See Fig2)
c) Pattern 4 Pedestal WC with  independent cis-

tern and concealed Strap  (See Fig.3)
3. Requirements
3.1 Each water closet shall be  provided with not less
than four fixing holes having a minimum diameter of
6.5mm.

3.2 Flushing rim may be box rim  or  open rim type
flushing rim and the inlet shall be  of self draining type.

3.3 Integral trap with P or S outlet

3.4 Anti-siphonage vent loinat an angle of 450 where
required by sanitation authority.

Note— For detailed dimensions and tolerance refer to 5
of the standard.

4.  Flushing Tests

4.1 Toilet Paper Test — On repetition of test for the
pan shall discharge full charge of the paper at least
thrice.

5.2 Smudge Test — Immediately after the flushing, there
shall be no smudge left on the bowl.

5.3 Holding Capacity Test — Shall be capable of
holding not less than 10 litres of water between the
normal water level and the highest possible water level
of the water closet as installed.

SQUATTING PANS
(Fourth Revision)

1. Scope —  Requirements for patterns, sizes,
construction, dimensions, finish, flushing tests,
inspection and marking for vitreous squatting pans.

2. Requirements

2.1 Patterns and sizes

a) Long 580 and 630 mm ( See Fig.1)

b) Orissa 580 × 440 and 630 × 450 mm (See Fig.1)

c) Rural 480 mm (See Fig 3)
Note — For detailed dimension and tolerances refer to 6 of
the  standard.

Note —   Foot rest may be flushed or raised, clearance
permissible between raised footrest and rim opening.

FIG. 1  LONG  PATTERN  SQUATTING  PAN

2.2 Each pan except the rural pattern shall have  an
integral flushing rim of the suitable.

2.3 Pan of 630 mm shall be of box rim type. Pan of sizes
smaller than 630 mm may be either box rim or   open rim
type. The flushing rim and inlet shall be of self draining
type.

2.4 Trap with P or  S outlet (withor without inspection
vent ) for long and orissa pattern. The trap shall conform
to  IS  2556 ( Part 13) : 1973+.

3. Flushing Tests

3.1 Toilet Paper Test — When repeated four times, the
pan shall discharge full charge of paper at least thrice.
3.2 Smudge Test — Immediately after flushing there
shall be no smudge left on the pan.
3.3 Water Holding Capacity Test — Shall be capable
of holding not less than 10 litres of water between the
normal water level and highest possible water level of
the pan installed.

Cyclic test – Specimens are immersed in water at 27 ± 2ºC for a period of 72 h, followed b drying in air at 27 ± 2ºC for 24 h and
then heating in dry air at 70ºC for 72 h. There such cycles are to be followed and then the specimens are tested for internal bond
strength.

Accelerated water resistance test – Specimens are immersed in water 27 ± 2ºC and water is brought to boiling and kept at boiling
temperature for 2 hours. Specimens are then cooled in water 27 ± 2ºC and then tested for internal bond strength.

chunks.title: IS 13620  : 1993 FUSION BONDED  EPOXY COATED REINFORCING BARS
chunks.content:
IS 13620  : 1993 FUSION BONDED  EPOXY COATED
REINFORCING BARS

(First Revision)

1. Scope –  Covers deformed steel reinforcing bars 5. Application of Coating—The coating shall  be
with protective epoxy coating applied by electrostatic applied as an electrostatically charged dry powder
spray method. sprayed onto the grounded steel bar using an electrostatic
2. Coating Materials spray gun . The powder may be applied to either a hot

or cold bar. The coated bar shall be given a thermal
2.1 The coating material shall meet the requirements treatment specified by the manufacturer of the epoxy
specified in Annex A of the standard. resin which will provide a fully. cured finish coating.

Temperature shall  be controlled to ensure a workman
2.2 The patching or repairing material or both, shall be like job without blistering or other defects.
compatible with the coating, inert in concrete and
feasible for repairs at the coating plant or in the field. 6. Requirements  of Coated Bars

3. Reinforcing Steel 6.1 Coating Thickness – For acceptance purposes at
Steel reinforcing bars to be coated shall conform to least 90 percent of all   coating thickness measurement

IS 1786 : 1985* shall be 0.1 mm to 0.3 mm after curing. The coating
thickness limits do not apply to patch areas.

4. Surface Preparation
6.2 Continuity of Coating – The coating shall be

4.1 The surface of the steel reinforcing bars to be coated visually inspected after curing for continuity of the
shall be cleaned by abrassive blast cleaning to near white coating and shall be free from holes, voids,
metal. The surface profile shall be free from mill scale contamination, cracks and damaged areas discernible
,rust and foreign matter when viewed under well-lit to the unaided eye. In addition, there shall be not more
conditions. than an average of two holidays per 300 mm
4.2 The coating shall be applied to the cleaned surface Note – Holiday means a pin hole not discernible to the unaided
as soon as possible after cleaning. Any formation of eye.
rust blooms on the cleaned bars are to be removed by 6.3  Adhesion –  No visible   cracks or disbonding in
blast cleaning before application of the coating. the  coating on the outside radius shall be allowed.
However, in no case shall the coating be delayed more
than eight hours after  cleaning.

* Plain high  strength deformed steel bars, and wires for concrete
reinforcement (third revision).

For detailed information, refer to IS  13620 : 1993  Fusion bonded epoxy coated reinforceing bars (first revision).

4.2 Elongation – The total elongation under load shall not be less than 3.5 percent on a minimum gauge length of

breaking strength of the strand shall have relaxation losses of not more than 1.8 percent after 100 h and not more
than 2.5 percent after 1000 h.

standard.

No flashover or breakdown between electrodes shall
occur before a total of 50 drops has fallen. Test

Protection against electric shock
Note– 1. Care is taken that the electrodes are clean, correctly Provision for earthing
shaped and correctly positioned before each test is started.
2. In case of doubt the test is repeated, if necessary, on a new
set of samploes.

4. Tests –

Note – For  detail requirements refer to the standard

Rigid plain conduits of insulation materials.

IS 875 (PART 1) : 1987 CODE OF PRACTICE FOR DESIGN  LOADS
(OTHERTHAN EARTHQUAKE)FORBUILDINGSANDSTRUCTURES,

PART-1 DEAD LOADS – UNIT WEIGHTS OF BUILDING
MATERIALS AND STORED MATERIALS.

1. Scope – Covers unit weight mass of materials, and Felt, bituminous for water-proofing and
parts or components in a building that apply to the damp-proofing
determination of dead loads in the design of buildings. Foam slag, foundry pumice
The unit weight mass of materials that are likely to be Glass
stored in a building are also specified in the standard
for the purpose of load calculations along with angles Gutter, asbestos cement
internal friction as appropriate. Gypsum

Iron
